sections.body_text:

Fig. S3. Mutations in the evolved KE59 variants. Shown is the designed KE59 model, with the side-chains that were mutated in the evolved variants. The substrate (magenta) is overlayed from the KE59 design. (A) Residues of consensus mutations (green sticks). (B) Residues at protein surface distant from the active site (orange). (C) Residues in the active site $\beta$ strands (cyan). (D) Residues in the loops at the top of the active site (yellow).

Fig. S4. Heat denaturation of KE59 variants. Shown is the residual activity after incubating the KE59 variants at $5 5 { - } 9 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 15 minutes and cooling to room temperature. Enzymatic activity was measured by initial rates with $0 . 2 5 \mathrm { m M }$ 5- nitrobenzisoxazole, and the reported results are the average of two independent measurements. Note the lack of correlation between the apparent melting temperatures of KE59 variants obtained from this heat denaturation experiment and their soluble expression (Fig. S2). For example, the thermal stability of variants R8-2/7A and R9-1/4A appears to be inferior to that of the design, but their soluble expression is much higher.

\* In the libraries, the NdeI site in the synthetic gene encoding the designed KE59 protein was replaced by NcoI, which involved insertion of alanine residue after the N-terminus methionine. Mutagenesis level was regulated by varying the template concentration and number of polymerization cycles in the error-prone PCR with mutazyme (Genemorph PCR mutagenesis kit, Stratagene). The number of mutations per gene was estimated by sequencing ${ \sim } 1 0$ clones from the unscreened library, and is indicated in the second column. It should be noted that only a fraction of the obtained diversity $( > 5 , 0 0 0$ different permutations, even at a single amino acid mutation per variant) could be covered by our medium throughput screening (600-2000 variants were screened at each directed evolution round). † Since the expression of KE59 had not reached its maximal level, the apparent improvement in activity was often due to an increase in expression rather than specific activity.

sections.title: B. Kinetic analysis of KE59 variants
sections.body_text:
Enzymatic characterization. For the kinetic characterization, the reactions were started by adding $1 5 0 ~ \mu \mathrm { l }$ of substrate (final concentration - $0 . 1 3 \mathrm { - } 1 . 0 5 ~ \mathrm { m M }$ , or $0 . 0 6 5 { \cdot } 0 . 5 2 5 \mathrm { m M }$ , in $2 5 ~ \mathrm { m M }$ HEPES $\mathrm { p H } ~ 7 . 2 5$ with $1 0 0 \ \mathrm { m M \ N a C l } )$ , to $5 0 \mu \mathrm { l }$ of enzyme in storage buffer, as previously described (2, 3).

$p H \cdot$ -rate profile. $\mathbf { k } _ { \mathrm { c a t } }$ and $\mathrm { K } _ { \mathrm { m } }$ values were determined with 5-nitrobenzisoxazole at $\mathrm { p H } ~ 5 \textmd { - }$ 8.5 for KE59 variants R2-4/3D, R4-5/11B, R8-2/7A, R9-1/4A, and R13-3/11H (2, 3). Initial velocities $\left( \mathbf { v } _ { 0 } \right)$ were determined at eight different substrate concentrations (0.13- $1 . 0 5 ~ \mathrm { m M }$ or $0 . 0 6 5 { \cdot } 0 . 5 2 5 \mathrm { m M } )$ . The buffers used (at $5 0 ~ \mathrm { m M }$ ) were: Citrate (pH 5.0-5.5), MES $\mathrm { ( p H ~ } 5 . 5 – 6 . 5 )$ , and Bis-tris propane $\mathrm { ( p H ~ 6 . 5  – 8 . 0 ) }$ at $5 0 ~ \mathrm { m M }$ . At $\mathrm { p H } 5 . 5$ and 6.5, the activity was measured with both relevant buffers. The ionic strength was adjusted to a total of 0.1M with NaCl.

Data analysis. Kinetic parameters $( k _ { c a t } , K _ { M } , k _ { c a t } / K _ { M } )$ were obtained by fitting the data to the Michaelis-Menten equation $[ \mathrm { v } _ { 0 } { = } k _ { c a t } [ \mathrm { E } ] _ { 0 } [ \mathrm { S } ] _ { 0 } / ( [ \mathrm { S } ] _ { 0 } { + } K _ { M } ) ] .$ , using the program Kaleidagraph 5.0. When working with low substrate concentrations, data were fitted to the linear regime of the Michaelis-Menten model $[ \mathrm { v _ { 0 } } \mathrm { = [ S ] _ { 0 } [ E ] _ { 0 } k _ { c a t } / K _ { M } } ]$ , and $\mathrm { k _ { c a t } / K _ { M } }$ was deduced from the slope. All the data presented are the averages of at least three independent experiments, with the standard deviations between the repeated experiments typically being $< 1 0 \%$ .

Fig. S5. Product formation was monitored with $0 . 2 5 \mathrm { m M }$ 5-nitrobenzisoxazole (A), 0.1mM 5,7- dichlorobenzisoxazole $\mathbf { ( B ) }$ , $0 . 1 \mathrm { m M }$ 6-chlorobenzisoxazole (C) and KE59 variants at various protein concentrations $\mathbf { \eta } ^ { ( \mathbf { D } ) }$ . Also shown is buffer/hydroxide catalyzed decomposition (no enzyme). Multiple turnovers were observed in all the cases, and saturation was observed with activated substrates (5-nitro and 5,7-dichloro benzisoxazoles). In case of less activated substrate (6-chlorobenzisoxazole), saturation was observed with the more active KE59 variants, but not for KE59 from earlier rounds of directed evolution. Possible reasons for this could be protein destabilization and inactivation over the long reaction time and/or product inhibition.

sections.title: C. Structural analysis of KE59 variants
Protein crystallization, data collection, and refinement.
sections.body_text:
KE59 variants subjected to crystallization were purified by Ni-NTA HiTrap chelating HP column (GE), followed by gel filtration (HiLoad 16/60 Superdex $7 5 ^ { \mathrm { T M } }$ , GE). Crystals of KE59 variants were obtained by the sitting-drop, and the hanging-drop vapor diffusion and microbtach under oil methods using the Mosquito robot (TTP LabTech, Inc., Cambridge, MA, USA). The protein concentration used for crystallization of all variants was $2 0 { - } 3 5 ~ \mathrm { m g / m l }$ and all the crystals were grown at $4 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ . Diffraction data were integrated, scaled, and reduced using the HKL2000 program package (4). The structure of R8-2/7A apo KE59 variant was solved by molecular replacement with the program PHASER (5), using the refined structure of indoleglycerolphosphate synthase from Sulfolobus Solfataricus (PDB entry 1IGS) as a model. The structures of the remaining KE59 variants were solved using the R8-2/7A apo KE59 variant structure as a model. All the steps of atomic refinement were carried out with the program CCP4/Refmac5 (6). The models were built to !a-weighted, $2 \mathrm { F _ { o b s } }$ $- \operatorname { F } _ { \mathrm { c a l c } }$ , and $\mathrm { F _ { o b s } - F _ { c a l c } }$ maps using the program COOT (7). Details of the data collection and structure refinement are described in Table S7. The KE59 models were evaluated with the program PROCHECK!"#.

sections.title: D. Molecular Dynamics analysis of KE59 variants
sections.body_text:
Materials and Methods. Molecular Dynamics simulations were carried out on the wild type KE59 design (KE59wt), and the evolved variants R1-7/10H, R2-4/3D, R4-6/6A, R5- 11/5F, R8-2/7A, R9-1/4A, and R13-3/11H. Each variant was prepared for nine independent MD simulations: one apo-MD, and two MDs for each of the substrates 5-nitro-6-chlorobenzisoxazole, 5-nitro-benzisoxazole, 5,7-dichloro-benzisoxazole, and 4,6-dichlorobenzisoxazole. These were initiated with the substrates in two distinct orientations – one with the heterocycle pointing towards the bottom of the active site (as anticipated from the computational design) and one with the heterocycle pointing towards bulk solvent (corresponding to a 180 degree rotation). Substrate parameters were generated within the antechamber module of AMBER 10 (9) using the general AMBER force field, with partial charges set to fit the electrostatic potential generated at $\mathrm { H F } / 6 { - } 3 1 \mathrm { G } ^ { * }$ by RESP (10). The charges were calculated according to the Merz-Singh-Kollman scheme (11, 12) using Gaussian 03 (13). The structures immersed in a truncated octahedral box with a 10 Å buffer of TIP3P(14) water molecules. The systems were neutralized by addition of explicit counter ions. All simulations were carried out using the Stony Brook modification of the Amber 99 force field (15). A two stage geometry optimization approach was utilized, initially minimizing the positions of water molecules and ions, followed by an unrestrained minimization of all atoms. The systems were heated gently in six 50K, 50ps steps from 0 to 300K at constant volume periodic boundary conditions. Harmonic restraints of $3 0 \mathrm { k c a l / m o l }$ were applied to the solute, and the Langevin equilibration scheme was used to control and equalize the temperature. The time step was kept at 1 fs during the heating stages, allowing potential inhomogeneities to self-adjust. Each system was then equilibrated for 2 ns with a 2 fs timestep at a constant pressure of 1 atm. Water molecules were triangulated with the SHAKE algorithm such that the angle between the hydrogen atoms is kept fixed. A 20 ns production MD simulation was performed for each of the systems using PMEMD (16). Geometries and velocities were saved every 100 steps (0.2 ps) which resulted in a total of 100,000 frames from each production run. Long-range electrostatic effects were modeled using the particle-mesh-Ewald method (17).Post-MD data extraction and analysis were performed using the ptraj module of AMBER 10 (18)

Arrangement of E230, S210 and W109 in the active site. W109 and S210 are in direct competition for E230, and these are mutually exclusive contacts. The interaction between E230 and W109 has a dual effect: on one hand, it can fixate the positioning of E230 and draw its carboxylate oxygens away from S210, which coincides with increased $\mathbf { k } _ { \mathrm { c a t } }$ and $\mathrm { k _ { c a t } / K _ { m } }$ values (Fig. S13). On the other hand, the interaction between E230 and W109 can also weaken the basicity of E230. QM transition state calculations show that W109, when bound to one of the carboxylate oxygens of E230, can increase the reaction barrier by up to $1 . 5 \mathrm { k c a l / m o l }$ (Fig. S14).

Fig. S15. Designed substrate orientation (O1) versus alternative substrate orientation (O2): differential analysis of $2 6 \times 2 \mathrm { ~ M D ~ }$ trajectories. (A) Differential analysis of substrate positioning: $\Delta$ (substrate $\mathrm { R M S D } ) = \mathrm { R M S D } ( \mathrm { O 1 } ) - \mathrm { R M S D } ( \mathrm { O 2 } )$ ; average $\Delta$ is $0 . 0 2 \mathrm { ~ \AA ~ }$ . $\mathbf { ( B ) }$ Differential analysis of water accessibility: $\Delta$ (# waters) $=$ #wat(O1) - #wat(O2); average $\Delta$ is 0.22 water molecules. Dotted line: average of full dataset; Solid line: average of the individual substrate series; Dashed line: average standard deviation of full dataset $( \sigma )$ . A value of 0 corresponds to a O1:O2 ratio of 1:1. Positive values beyond $\sigma$ correspond to a bias towards O1; negative values beyond $\sigma$ correspond to a bias towards O2. The data suggests that no significant bias exists for either O1 or O2 with any of the four substrates.

sections.title: E. Determination of kuncat for Kemp elimination of various benzisoxazoles
sections.body_text: kuncat was determined as a function of buffer concentration at $\mathrm { p H } ~ 7 . 2 5$ at $2 5 \mathrm { { ^ \circ C } }$ , and extrapolated to zero buffer concentration. Non-enzymatic elimination was followed in Hepes $\mathrm { p H } ~ 7 . 2 5$ (10, 25, 50, 75, 100mM) by monitoring the absorbance. For 5- nitrobenzisoxazole and 5,7-dichlorobenzisoxazole, the plots were fit to the equation: OD $= \mathrm { C \ ( 1 { - } e ^ { - \mathrm { k t } } ) }$ , where OD is the absorbance, C is a constant, $\mathrm { k }$ is the ${ \mathrm { k } } _ { \mathrm { u n c a t : } }$ , and $\mathrm { \Delta t }$ is time in minutes. For 6-chloro and 5-fluorobenzisoxazole, the initial linear stage of elimination was monitored. The kinetics of elimination at various buffer concentrations are represented at Fig. S19-S22. The ${ \bf k } _ { \mathrm { u n c a t } }$ values for zero buffer concentration were obtained by plotting the ${ \bf k } _ { \mathrm { u n c a t } }$ values obtained for various Hepes concentrations and extrapolating to $0 \mathrm { m M }$ Hepes (Fig. S23). The obtained ${ \bf k } _ { \mathrm { u n c a t } }$ values are summarized in Table S9.

sections.title: F. Synthesis and characterization of substrates and inhibitors
sections.body_text: Benzisoxazole, benzotriazole, and 6-chlorobenzotriazole were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. 5-nitrobenzotriazole was purchased from Matrix Organics. 5-nitrobenzisoxazole was prepared by nitration of benzisoxazole (19). All the other benzisoxazoles were prepared from corresponding salicylaldehydes by the procedure of Kemp (20). $p K _ { a } ^ { L G }$ values of the salicylonitriles were obtained from the literature (Table S10).

sections.body_text:
5-chlorosalicylaldehyde (0.5gr, 3.2mmol) was dissolved in ethanol. Hydroxylammonium O-sulfonate $( 0 . 5 \mathrm { g r } )$ was added, and the mixture was stirred $1 0 \mathrm { { m i n } }$ at RT. Then, $1 5 \mathrm { m l }$ DCM was added, and the reaction was cooled at $4 \mathrm { { } ^ { \circ } C }$ . Solution of $\mathrm { N a H C O } _ { 3 }$ was added $( 0 . 7 5 \mathrm { g r / 7 m l \ H _ { 2 } O ) }$ , and the reaction mixture was mixed for $3 0 \mathrm { { m i n } }$ at RT. After fractions separation, the aqueous fraction was extracted with 3 times with DCM. The DCM fractions were combined, dried with ${ \mathrm { N a } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { S O } } _ { 4 }$ , filtered and evaporated. The residue was purified by flash chromatography (silica 60, $2 \%$ MeOH in DCM).

Kinetic parameters $( k _ { c a t } , ~ K _ { M } , ~ k _ { c a t } / K _ { M } )$ were obtained by fitting the data to the Michaelis-Menten equation $[ \mathrm { v } _ { 0 } { = } k _ { c a t } [ \mathrm { E } ] _ { 0 } [ \mathrm { S } ] _ { 0 } / ( [ \mathrm { S } ] _ { 0 } { + } K _ { M } ) ] .$ , using the program Kaleidagraph 5.0. Fig. S14. QM/QM’ transition state (TS) calculations of R13-3/11H variant with 5-nitrobenzisoxazole. (A) TS with E230-W109 contact. (B) TS without E230-W109 contact. Sticks were computed at the DFT $\mathsf { b 3 l y p } / 6 \mathrm { - } 3 1 \mathrm { + g ( d , p ) }$ level of theory, and lines were treated with the semiempirical PM3MM using the 2-layer ONIOM approach implemented in Gaussian03. The computed TS values are relative to the R13-3/11H:substrate complex.
Fig. S16. Correlation of increased catalytic activity with increased desolvation of E230.

Fig. S17. Geometric deviations from the ideal catalytic arrangement plotted against (A) $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } }$ and (B) $k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } / K _ { \mathrm { { m } } }$ . Root mean square deviations (RMSDs) are computed for the relative geometry between E230, W109, and the substrate from all MD frames. An RMSD of $0 \textup { \AA }$ corresponds to the ideal geometry for catalysis, derived from quantum mechanical optimizations (Fig. S14).

(C) Average atomic fluctuation of all labeled active site residues. Error bars correspond to $+ / -$ the standard deviation of the MD-based distributions.
